title: Too Shy for the Press
summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program opens the Civil Rights History Project interview in New York City with Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge), who recalls enrolling at Howard around age sixteen where cousins had gone before her, being thought shy her first year away from a small town, and studying under radical minds like the sociologist E. Franklin Frazier and Rayford W. Logan.

title: One Voice to Speak Through
summary: Richardson describes her first protests at Howard, against the university's curfew rules for women and picketing Woolworths around her junior year, and how, the self-described shy student, she ended up at a press conference only because the others wanted one voice through which to speak, crediting Frazier's attacks on the Black bourgeoisie for shaping her view.

title: The Third Black Man on the Council
summary: She situates her family as in effect the first Black family in Cambridge, Maryland, with her grandfather the third Black man on the City Council, reputedly wealthy, who gave money and land to the church, while her grandmother distributed Red Cross food and clothing to poor and seasonal workers from their house, and most relatives lived within a block and were raised to come back and help the town.

title: They Brought the Meal Out to Him
summary: Richardson describes a totally segregated Cambridge with one reserved maternity room in the hospital and a single Black ward, her grandfather consulted on credit and on getting people out of the Maryland penitentiary yet served his council banquet meal apart because he could not join the white members, and her late realization, reading The Help, of how deep such separations ran.

title: Doctors Without Privileges
topic: Health Care and Her Father's Store
summary: She recounts that Black doctors, including her cousin Carroll, lacked hospital privileges so the family traveled to Provident Hospital in Baltimore, distrusting Johns Hopkins as a place that used Black patients as guinea pigs, tells of her father, a druggist who worked until midnight before a heart attack in his fifties took him, and describes taking over the drugstore after his death while struggling to find a Black pharmacist and sending her then husband to Howard's pharmacy school.

title: Cookies on Saturdays
summary: She tells how SNCC field workers and Freedom Riders had been meeting at her uncle's house for nearly a month in late 1961 before she knew, how she let her daughters and other young people gather there on Saturdays to make cookies and guide the organizers around the community, and how she began as an observer watching over the youth on picket lines.

title: Put Off the Picket Line
summary: Richardson recounts her cousin Frederick St. Clair telling Freedom Riders that Cambridge needed fixing, organizers Bill Hansen and Reginald Robinson arriving, her daughter Donna organizing students who nearly stopped the town, her own daughter putting her off the picket line for confronting people who spat at the marchers, and the mass meeting that asked her to lead the new adult group because she would not be financially jeopardized, after which she organized through laundromats, churches, and bars.

part: The Survey and the Real Demands
title: White Gloves Meet SNCC
topic: From Voter Registration to a Survey
summary: Richardson recalls going to Atlanta in white gloves to meet the SNCC crew, her doubts about voter registration since Cambridge Black people had voted since the eighteen-hundreds yet still faced segregated hospitals, housing, and jobs, and how Junior CNAC of grammar school children carried a survey through the community that Swarthmore volunteers wrote up and correlated for their professors.

part: The Survey and the Real Demands
title: Public Accommodations at the Bottom
summary: She explains that the survey, built on the graduate methods she learned from E. Franklin Frazier, showed public accommodations ranked at the bottom of community priorities behind housing, jobs, and health, that Robert Kennedy grew sympathetic partly through the Irish experience of poverty, sending her to his housing and other agency commissions, and that secretary Weaver would put a shovel in the ground once they left the streets.

part: The Survey and the Real Demands
title: Chicken Coops and the Packing Plant
summary: Richardson describes people living in actual chicken coops stacked one on another with rent collected on them, roughly sixty percent unemployment, the Phillips Packing fuel magnate ordered not to send his trucks into the Black community who vowed to send them under Maryland State Police guard, and how Cambridge eventually ignored the middle-class NAACP whose lawyers would not represent ordinary people.

part: The Survey and the Real Demands
title: The Heart of CNAC
summary: She and the interviewer note that Cambridge's nearness to Washington drew heavy national press and a stream of job offers meant to deflect her leadership, including a state senate candidacy and a Carnegie Hall event with King and a Johnson aide, and she describes the ten to fourteen core CNAC members who, on her grandfather's model, were the recognized leaders in every neighborhood across the county.

title: If You Can Beat Us, Defend Us
summary: Richardson reflects on leading as a woman, attributing her freedom to being a long-awaited grandchild in a family where women were never told they could not act, and describes a division of labor in which men worked by day while women conducted movement business and stood guard, recalling women on the street challenging men who fought on Saturday night to instead stop the police from coming through their community.

title: Secret Whistles in the Field
summary: She recounts her daughter Donna crossing the field paths back home guided by the men's secret whistles in hunting country, a night when guardsmen or fired men drove through shooting up the Black community and were fired back upon, a machine gun someone had brought home from the war, and the press hiding under tables in her uncle's house as the smoke of gun ozone filled Pine Street by morning.

title: Read at Fort Belvoir
summary: Richardson describes National Guard commander General George Gelston, whom Black Cambridge came to respect as fair though willing to arrest, and the irony that both of them knew their phones were tapped, which is why he sent his second-in-command back and forth with messages rather than trusting lines that were routed all the way to Fort Belvoir to be read before being sent on as if normal.

part: The Referendum and the White House
title: Born Here, Not Voting on It
summary: She lays out her stand against the referendum on public accommodations, arguing that people whose Cambridge roots ran three hundred years through slavery and freedom should not have to vote on whether they could enter a restaurant, a position she found normal but that the NAACP, the Baltimore ministers, and others outside Cambridge opposed, perhaps thinking it made more sense against the Deep South.

part: The Referendum and the White House
title: The Word Went Out We'd Been Killed
summary: Richardson recounts a near clash of hundreds of Black and white people on Race Street that frightened the authorities, being summoned to the White House through General Gelston and family friend Maceo Hubbard of the civil rights division, police cars stationed along every crossing from Cambridge to Washington for safety, a possible shot mistaken for a backfire, and returning to find press in her living room because word had spread she was killed.

title: Like a Hog Against the Wall
summary: She reflects on managing fear, saying she was not fearless but trusted that if anyone got her the people of Cambridge would get them, and that she would recite a paraphrase of Claude McKay, that if pinned against the wall like a hog she would not die like one, repeating it as she walked through the valley of the shadow.

title: A One-Cent Fine
summary: Richardson tells of her mother arrested for trying to be served at a Coca-Cola counter, her daughter Donna and young people arrested then beaten when they changed into jeans and lay in at the theaters, the whole town pouring out by midnight in nightgowns until the jails filled and surrounding towns refused the overflow, and a conflicted Judge Lord Henry handing down a one-cent fine while lecturing her about her grandfather.

title: The First Civilian Tear Gas
summary: She recalls the Citizens Council bringing George Wallace to speak at the segregated fire department that Black residents had helped fund yet could not enter, a march that drew people of every background, and the use of CS gas reported as the first civilian use of that gas, after which an elderly man and an infant died, people crawled on the ground, and the arrestees still carried the gas in their clothes to Baltimore County.

title: It Turned Into a Big Party
summary: Richardson describes being asked weeks before the March on Washington to add women to the program, told not to wear jeans so she searched the Eastern Shore for a denim skirt with straps, her sense that the march was turning into a party while Cambridge people were threatened in the streets, and arriving as a SNCC coordinator for the Eastern Shore to find the women being held in a holding tent.

title: They Took Your Chair
summary: She tells how Bayard Rustin found her waiting near a satellite broadcast and brought her through the crowd to the stage where Lena Horne and Josephine Baker sat, how she realized the whole thing was planned when she was told her chair had been removed, slipped to the back to talk with attorney Robert Ming about a Cambridge legal matter, and helped present Rosa Parks to the international media.

title: Powell Got the Food In
summary: Richardson names Adam Clayton Powell Jr., Robert Kennedy, and Malcolm X among those she still cannot watch on television, and describes Powell arranging surplus food for Cambridge over the City Council and governor who denied anyone was starving, the Guard distributing it, and Black men loading vans to carry food to white families threatened off welfare for showing up at the distribution sites.

title: A Union at the Rod and Gun Club
summary: She recounts an effort, in the middle of all the racial tension, to organize a garment workers union at Phillips Packing Company where lower wages were paid for the same work, Black and white workers meeting at a Black rod and gun club after being blocked from white facilities, and Black men being asked to voice the complaints to the New York organizers, as had happened when SNCC went to Appalachia.

title: At Reverend Cleage's Church
summary: Richardson describes going to Detroit for Reverend C.L. Franklin's SCLC event, being told she was in the wrong place and that Malcolm X was speaking at Reverend Albert Cleage's church, hearing him invoke the Honorable Elijah Muhammad in a tone that made her sense he would not stay in the Nation of Islam long, and watching the Cambridge clinic owner Bob Pannett turn completely around over Malcolm.

title: He Should Make It Clear
summary: She recalls inviting Malcolm X to Cambridge, urging that before the election with a candidate named Goldwater running it should be the ballot rather than the bullet, to which he agreed, being up at the Audubon Ballroom and meant to be there the day he was killed, and her mother having offered Malcolm's family a third-floor parsonage in New Jersey after his house was bombed.

title: His Biggest Mistake
summary: Richardson disputes Manning Marable's biography, which she finds patronizing and has not finished past 1962, reportedly quoting a review that Malcolm's biggest mistake was appearing on stage with her, rejecting the word reinvent in favor of grew, and reflecting that she was glad the press came to Cambridge because they eventually learned and carried the community's issues.

title: Keep Us In or Protect Us
summary: She and the interviewer frame her as a uniquely independent national movement leader, and she credits her family for teaching her to judge people not by titles so she felt no awe of important people, her independence drawn from the determination of the people she represented and the creative chaos sustained for months, and recalls the shifting belief about whether the Guard was there to keep them in or to protect them.

title: They Don't Have the People in the Street
summary: Richardson recounts that SNCC let local people decide and did not interfere, that James Forman could not order her to drop Malcolm X and let one phone call settle it, and that when the national organizations debated Malcolm she had to tell them they had no standing because they did not have the people in the street, recalling Roy Wilkins, who had known her family, trying to talk her out of her position.

title: Tell the Kennedys to Go to Hell
summary: She recalls sending the Kennedy brothers a defiant message through Maceo Hubbard, sitting on a step at her uncle's with the telephone, crediting General Gelston over his harsher superior with keeping things from getting bloodier, and describing Gelston walking unbidden into a City Council meeting and calmly offering to leave when challenged, proving he held real power with someone.

title: An FBI Informant in the House
summary: Richardson identifies a figure who moved through CNAC, the NAACP, and a Swarthmore-linked group as an FBI informant exposed in documents from the Media, Pennsylvania FBI office break-in, and tells of Stokely Carmichael ringing the bell at three in the morning on his way through, the informant grabbing a gun kept in the house against intruders, a moment unsettling only in hindsight.

title: Six or Seven Lost Months
summary: She judges that the movement accomplished what it wanted yet puzzles over a gap after she left when SNCC people went to Atlantic City and the schools were desegregated and a housing project begun, wondering whether incompetence cost six or seven months, notes white Cambridge electing a racist county figure before steps toward desegregation, and marvels that the town now has a Black woman mayor whose father sat on her executive board.

title: This Was Not a Paid Position
summary: Richardson explains moving to New York City in 1965 because the man she married lived there, the agreement had been signed, leadership remained in Cambridge, and she had two daughters to raise, stressing that hers was never a salaried position with offices and secretaries and that she had not meant to spend the rest of her life at it, hoping the people had at least learned they could fight back.

title: The Whole Town Was Involved
summary: She recalls a recent Cambridge tribute that overwhelmed her, the whole town involved in a service and an Elks home banquet before a mixed audience, awards from government she had not expected and the newly elected mayor, and an earlier honor at Washington College whose president had learned of Cambridge through Malcolm X's Message to the Grassroots, where she found scholarships given to local Kent County students.

title: Going to Hell in a Handbasket
summary: Richardson closes by saying the country is going to hell in a handbasket, pointing to a far-right Supreme Court she weighs even against slavery, warning her citizenship class students that what was done to Black people is spreading to everyone, faulting a movement that let the Tea Party get too far out with no opposing entity outside academia, before the interviewer thanks her and closes the recording.
